title: 2025/11/06 - TI
description: Thursday 6 November 2025 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee Ministry of Transport Te Manatū Waka, Long-term Insights Briefing 2025, Moving people in 2055 Hearing of Evidence: Ministry of Transport Ruth Fairhall, Chief Executive Paul O’Connell, DCE Sector Strategy Dan Jenkins, Director – Data Improvement Jade Mackay, Chief Transport Modeller Mac Townsend, Senior Data Analyst – National Transport Modelling Chris Nees, Chief of Staff Katie Collier, Principal Adviser – Strategic Priorities
descriptionSummary: Transport and Infrastructure Committee. Ministry of Transport Te Manatū Waka, Long-term Insights Briefing 2025, Moving people in 2055. Ministry of Transport

chunks.text: We have to,

Right. Good morning. Morning to see you. Nice to see some familiar faces there too. Um, so we've got the, uh, we've got the board and all background material fitness. So if there's anything you want to particularly highlight, uh, but I think it could be some rich conversation going on some of the, uh, material. And I noticed that it was described as being, uh, about, um, uh, autonomous vehicles or robocar, but actually I thought it was more about the Model Monte model, which I thought was the, the key, um, key take out

of, uh, out of all of that and what you can do with it. So anyway, uh, in, in your hands if you wanna do some intros and then, um, yeah. Thanks Mr. Chair. Mr. Chair. I'll just do a couple of intros. I'm Ruth Erhu, I'm acting secretary. Um, Paul O'Connell, who you know before as, um, DC, um, sector strategy, and both the modeling people and the, um, sector strategy people who wrote the Alta Burn this area.

And then we have Dan Jenkins, um, who's the data director of data improvement, also has some technical modeling people here. So if you get technical questions about the model, we have some people that can answer those questions now. Cool. Um, and I'll hand over to Paul. Thanks, Ruth. Thank you. Um, for, just bear with me. I'd just like to start with a bit of a, uh, talk about how we've chosen to focus on technology and population change.

You know, why, why this? Um, as was said in the Danish Parliament in the 1930s, predictions are hard, particularly about the future. Um, so of approach this, we took some advice from Confucius who said, uh, study the past, if you would define the future. So I'd like to start by going back 30 years, uh, which isn't that long in the life of our transport system.

Uh, back then I was at, uh, Burnside High in Christchurch. Uh, and the transport network then down there is very similar to the network. Now. You went down Memorial Ave, uh, to get to the city from the airport. That road's largely unchanged now from what it was then. Uh, and at that point it was 35 years old. Uh, my transport experience then would've been very similar to a teenager on Christchurch.

Today. I rode my bike to school. I rode my bike, uh, to work on the weekends through paths and roads that are largely the same. Um, if I go further back the roads, my grandfather road to get to work on the railways pages Road and Ferry road, they're still there. The railway lines are still there. Um, I expect the infrastructure commission will have mentioned this to you when they met with you, that most of our future infrastructure is infrastructure we already have today.

And when we look at the big transport infrastructure changes that have happened in Canterbury over the past 30 years, it shows how difficult those predictions are. Uh, and large number of the big investments down there, such as the motorway, up to roller stone, the state highway one realignment to running Cota, they came as a result of earthquakes that no one could have predicted.

The regulations that govern activity on the network, they're also very similar, uh, to what they were back then. They've been updated. But in broad form, our regulatory and infrastructure is similar to what it was. Then, however the population's changed, it was 330,000, then. It's now more than 400,000. Uh, Rolleston isn't in that number.

They've had 800%, uh, population growth over the same period. And if we look at transport technology back in 1995, that's changed a lot. I learned to drive 30 years ago in a dangerous and, uh, very unreliable mini lovely, though it was, um, my father had a mobile radio in his car, uh, where he would get radio calls from a service that picked up your phone when you weren't at home and then radioed them to you.

Uh, they've, I think they've gone bankrupt now. Um, my bicycle was steel and clunky, um, and our computer was an old Apple Macintosh that you couldn't do anything with on the network. However, the transport technology we use now was significantly different. Um, cars today are arguably closer to computers than they are to cars of the 1990s.

Uh, I've got bikes that have got electronic gearing, uh, radars on them. Uh, some with electric motors. Yesterday I booked flights to Australia with an app on my phone that's thousands of times more powerful than my computer from the 19th. Uh, and when it was in, in Australia last, I paid for all of my transport by tapping my phone on a, on a reader.

So that's what we've chosen to focus on in this. If we project out the population and use simulation technology that computing power has just made possible, which we started to use last year, what are the likely future outcomes? And how might new transport technology affect that? So we've got Monty, which is a digital trend of the, uh, national Land Transport Network.

We started to use it in 2024. Uh, and it simulates how mil millions of digital people move around, uh, New Zealand over the course of a day. There's no me in the model, but there is an anonymous digital person, 1 0 5 a who lives at my address, um, and goes to work somewhere in the city each day. Uh, moderny assesses the optimal way for that person to travel throughout their city on a, in a given scenario based on the modes and routes available to them.

The base cases calibrated to daily traffic and public transport use data to represent travel in New Zealand today. And we use it in this L tip to model two possible scenarios, a 2055 future with population change, uh, and an automated vehicle scenario. So it's not just trying to, uh, get people's mental models about this, right.

Mon Monty's not a model where you can just ask it a question like chat GPT, uh, and get an answer. So the, the simulations here took three days for the robo taxii, uh, simulation in Napier at a 10% population level. If, if we'd done it with a hundred percent population in ap, it would've taken three weeks of computing power.

So to protect project out to 2055, we had to create a bespoke 2055 population. Stats NZ projections don't go out that far. Uh, and when we run that model in 2055, we find that unsurprisingly, uh, as our population grows, pressure on infrastructure increases, uh, and traffic times increase, that's consistent with experiences in all countries as they grow.

Uh, and so what about technology? Will, will autonomous vehicles, uh, save us? Well, in modeling our hypothetical example in Napier? No, we don't think so. Uh, we think the introduction of automated vehicles is highly plausible. Um, 80% of travel in New Zealand Zealanders by car, uh, automated trials of underway in the USA China and the Middle East.

Um, but our analysis suggests that replacing private car use with, uh, roboto taxis doesn't alleviate congestion. In fact, it, it makes it worse. Uh, and this one, we find that vehicle kilometers travel actually increased 'cause the robot taxis have to move around without people in them between trips.

It seems obvious after having done the analysis, but it wasn't something we'd considered beforehand. It's sort underwhelming. Right. Uh, this tells us the transport system will face a range of pressures over the next 50 years, particularly in Auckland. Uh, we'll need to use a range of tools to address those pressures from new infrastructure like road and rail, uh, public transport services to demand management by the time of use Bill that, uh, you have just considered.
